variables.content: Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

Assume the true thermodynamic-limit single-quasiparticle band of a 2D HEG is a smooth function \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) in a neighbourhood of the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: fitting the finite-cell discrete DMC band values to the quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4}
      $$
      over a modest interval \\([k_{F}-\\Delta k,\\,k_{F}+\\Delta k]\\) (with \\(\\Delta k\\) chosen so that the interval is not extremely small and contains sufficient discrete \\(k\\)-points to characterise curvature) yields an estimate of the derivative \\(\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}\\) that is an adequately accurate approximation to the true thermodynamic-limit derivative for the purpose of computing the quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}=k_{F}/(d\\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{F}}\\), provided the quartic form captures the local curvature of \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\).

The fitted DMC bands deviate from a purely quadratic dependence on $k$: when the fitted quartic form $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$ is used, the quartic coefficient $\alpha_4$ is positive for the paramagnetic HEG at $r_s=5$ and $10$ a.u., while $\alpha_4$ is negative for the ferromagnetic HEG at all densities studied; because these fitted bands are non-quadratic, thermodynamic properties that depend on integrals over the dispersion (for example temperature-dependent contributions to the free energy and specific heat at elevated temperatures) are predicted to depart from the free-particle-like behavior that would follow from a strictly quadratic dispersion $\propto k^2$.

At each density, spin polarization, and finite system size the discrete DMC band values \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) (where \\(k=|\\mathbf{k}|\\)) were fitted over an extended wavevector interval to a quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4},
      $$
      with fitted coefficients \\(\\alpha_{0},\\alpha_{2},\\alpha_{4}\\). The quasiparticle effective mass was then obtained from the definition
      $$
      m^{*}=\\frac{k_{F}}{\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}},
      $$
      where \\(k_{F}\\) is the Fermi wavevector of the infinite 2D HEG at the given density and spin polarization and the derivative is evaluated at \\(k=k_{F}\\). It was observed that extracting the derivative from fits restricted to a very narrow interval immediately surrounding \\(k_{F}\\) yields ill-behaved (pathological) estimates of \\(m^{*}\\), whereas performing the quartic fit over a larger modest wavevector range around \\(k_{F}\\) produces stable, systematically varying estimates of \\(m^{*}\\). Averaging the band behaviour over such a modest \\(k\\)-range therefore provides a controlled practical procedure to suppress the finite-cell and Hartree–Fock-like near-\\(k_{F}\\) fluctuations that cause the pathological behaviour.

The extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses exhibit distinct density dependences depending on spin polarization: for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10 the extrapolated effective mass remains close to unity (\\(m^{*}\\approx1\\)), indicating no significant interaction-induced mass enhancement at these densities for the clean continuum 2D HEG model considered; by contrast, for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG the extrapolated effective mass decreases as the density is lowered, for example taking the values \\(0.85(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=1\\), \\(0.74(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=5\\), and \\(0.70(3)\\) at \\(r_{s}=10\\), a trend that is qualitatively consistent with experimental measurements on spin-polarized two-dimensional systems reported in the literature (e.g., Padmanabhan et al.).

The quasiparticle effective masses m^{*} reported in the literature from DMC fits to the single-particle energy band and the ground-state total-energy parametrizations reported in the literature as continuous functions E_{\mathrm{corr}}(r_s,\zeta) of density parameter r_s and spin polarization \zeta are mutually consistent within their stated uncertainties and can be combined algebraically via standard Fermi-liquid thermodynamic relations (for example F_1^{s} = m^{*}-1 for the paramagnetic HEG and evaluation of F_0^{s,a} from derivatives of E(r_s,\zeta)) to produce numerical estimates of the dimensionless Fermi liquid parameters F_l for the 2D HEG.
      Refs. 11,23,24

The specific‑heat effective mass obtained from the empirical fits has the form m^*(X,P)/m_3 = B / [1 + A_1 ξ + A_2 ξ^{5/3}] with ξ = X/(1+αX) and with fitted coefficients B, A_1, A_2 determined by the weighted least‑squares procedure; the resulting m^*(X,P) curves are weak functions of concentration for X ≲ 10% and, importantly, the numerical values of the fitted m^*(X,P) depend on the temperature interval used in the fit: fits performed over the full temperature range T ≤ 0.65 K give slightly different m^*(X) than fits restricted to the low‑temperature range T ≤ 0.2 K, and the low‑temperature fits are in agreement with independent specific‑heat determinations of the specific‑heat effective mass.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.
